name: Desert Mountain
short_description: BLM public land near Nephi offering excellent agate and jasper collecting on accessible desert slopes. Free rockhounding with a 25 lb daily limit.
description:
Desert Mountain is a productive BLM rockhounding site in Juab County, Utah, situated on public land with no permit requirements or fees. The area features scattered agate nodules and jasper specimens weathering from volcanic deposits across relatively gentle desert terrain. This location is ideal for both beginners and experienced rockhounders seeking quality quartz-based specimens in a straightforward outdoor setting.

The geology here reflects the Basin and Range province's complex volcanic history. During the Miocene epoch, extensive silica-rich volcanic activity and hydrothermal processes deposited minerals throughout this region. Agate nodules formed within cavities of ancient basalt and rhyolite flows, while jasper and chalcedony specimens resulted from silica precipitation in fractures and weathered zones. Over millions of years, erosion has exposed these colorful deposits on hillsides and wash banks.

What makes Desert Mountain accessible is the combination of free public access, no permit burden, and the ability to hike directly to productive areas. You will find specimens ranging from small nodules to larger pieces weathering naturally from the slopes. The desert environment means minimal vegetation obstruction, making it easy to spot colorful agate and jasper fragments.

Best collecting occurs after winter and spring weather when frost action loosens specimens from the host rock. The dry desert air means specimens remain well-preserved on the surface for extended periods. Bring sun protection and water, as shade is limited and daytime temperatures can be intense even in cooler months.
history: Desert Mountain sits within the Basin and Range physiographic province, where Miocene-age volcanic eruptions between 20 and 30 million years ago deposited extensive rhyolite and basalt flows rich in silica. Hydrothermal fluids moving through fractures in these volcanic rocks deposited agate, jasper, and chalcedony as secondary minerals, creating the colorful nodules and specimens found today. This region was prospected historically for silver and other metals, but the gem-quality agates and jasper here have long attracted recreational collectors.
tips: Search the eroded hillsides and dry wash banks where nodules naturally weather free from the host rock; agate and jasper specimens often appear as rounded or angular chunks with distinctive banding and color. Use a hand lens to examine weathered surfaces for chalcedony banding patterns and color variation - look for translucent or banded specimens in white, tan, red, and brown hues. Bring a small rock hammer or chisel if you plan to extract pieces still embedded in outcrop; however, most surface specimens break free easily by hand after winter frost cycles. Mark productive zones mentally and check them seasonally, as new material surfaces as erosion continues.
terrain_notes: Desert Mountain occupies high-desert terrain at approximately 5,200 to 5,600 feet elevation with open, sparse vegetation typical of the Great Basin. Slopes are moderate (15-30 degrees) with exposed rock outcrops and weathered debris fields. Walking surfaces consist of loose volcanic rock, caliche, and compacted desert soil. Most productive collecting areas are within a 0.5 to 1 mile walk from parking. Footing is uneven but not treacherous; sturdy hiking boots are recommended to protect ankles from sharp volcanic rock fragments.
best_season: October - April. The desert climate here features hot, dry summers (often exceeding 90 degrees Fahrenheit) and mild winters. Fall and spring provide ideal conditions with comfortable temperatures in the 50s-70s Fahrenheit and reduced sun exposure. Winter brings occasional frost that naturally loosens specimens, and spring rains can expose fresh material. Summer heat and glare make midday collecting uncomfortable and potentially hazardous.
written_directions: From Nephi, take US 6 south for approximately 10 miles toward Salina. Turn east onto a marked BLM access road (look for mile marker 185 vicinity). Follow this road roughly 3 miles; it becomes rougher and narrower as you approach the base of Desert Mountain. A pullout or informal parking area sits near the mouth of a small drainage. Park here and hike east and upslope toward visible outcrop areas. GPS coordinates: 39.781293, -112.594062 will direct you to the primary collecting zone.
road_conditions: BLM access road is unpaved, washboard in places, with high-clearance passenger vehicles passable in dry conditions. Not recommended after heavy rain. Last 1-2 miles become rougher; ATV or pickup truck preferred but not required for initial access.
parking_notes: Informal pullout or small cleared area at the base of the mountain near the access road terminus. Space for 3-5 vehicles. No facilities. High-clearance vehicle parking recommended to avoid road ruts.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation. No removal of rock larger than hand-sized unless naturally weathered free. Respect existing claims (look for claim markers). Leave no trash. Camp only at designated BLM campsites if staying overnight.
rules:
Personal use only
Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA
No commercial collection allowed
No motorized excavation
No hand tools in unmined areas without permission
Respect existing mining claims
Pack out all trash
Use designated campsites for overnight stays
nearest_services: Nephi (approximately 10 miles west) has gas stations, restaurants, groceries, and lodging. Salina (approximately 12 miles south) also offers basic services.
accessibility_notes: Relatively gentle terrain with moderate slopes accessible to most hikers in reasonable fitness. Unpaved BLM access road to parking area may require high-clearance vehicle. No wheelchair access. Sturdy hiking boots recommended due to sharp volcanic rock. Shade is minimal; sun exposure is significant.

faq.question: What types of agates and jasper can I find at Desert Mountain near Nephi?
faq.answer: Desert Mountain produces banded agates with tan, white, red, and brown coloring, along with quality jasper specimens. You will also find chalcedony and quartz nodules. Most specimens are small to medium-sized (fist-sized or smaller), with occasional larger pieces. Look for translucent banding and distinctive color patterns that indicate gem-quality material.

faq.question: Is rockhounding free at Desert Mountain, and do I need a permit?
faq.answer: Yes, rockhounding at Desert Mountain is completely free. It is public BLM land with no permit requirement. You only need to follow the 25 lb daily collection limit per federal law and respect the personal use only rule.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to rockhound at Desert Mountain in Utah?
faq.answer: October through April is ideal. Fall and winter bring comfortable temperatures (50s-70s Fahrenheit) and frost cycles that naturally loosen specimens from host rock. Summer heat exceeds 90 degrees and makes collecting uncomfortable. Spring rains can expose fresh material, but summer UV and glare reduce collecting efficiency.

faq.question: How difficult is the hike at Desert Mountain, and is it suitable for families with children?
faq.answer: Desert Mountain is beginner-friendly with moderate slopes and short hiking distances (typically under 1 mile to productive zones). The terrain is uneven volcanic rock, so sturdy shoes are important, but children ages 6 and up can handle the hike easily. The site works well for families new to rockhounding.

faq.question: What tools do I need to collect agates and jasper at Desert Mountain?
faq.answer: A small hand lens helps identify gem quality specimens. A rock hammer or chisel is useful if you find pieces still in outcrop, though most surface specimens come free by hand. Bring a collection bag, sun protection, plenty of water, and wear sturdy hiking boots to protect against sharp volcanic rock.

faq.question: Can I drive all the way to the collecting area at Desert Mountain, or do I need to hike from the parking area?
faq.answer: You can drive a high-clearance vehicle approximately 3 miles on the BLM access road from US 6 near Nephi, but passenger cars may struggle with washboard and ruts. Park at the informal pullout at the road terminus and hike east upslope toward visible outcrops. The walk to productive zones is typically 0.5 to 1 mile.

meta_title: Desert Mountain Rockhounding Utah - Free BLM Agate
meta_description: Find agate, jasper, and quartz at Desert Mountain near Nephi, Utah. Free BLM rockhounding, no permit required. Beginner-friendly site open year-round.
